content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
April 8, 1991


Kurt Hahn
Finance Director
City Hall-Administrative Offices
P.O. Box 578
126 Matheson Street
Healdsburg, CA 95448-0578

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-037

Dear Mr. Hahn:

You have requested advice concerning the application of the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹ Because your questions do not pertain to a specific
pending decision, we are treating your request as one for informal
advice.²

QUESTIONS

1. You are considering investing city funds in a local
bank. One of your city councilmembers is a major investor and
director of the bank. Does the councilmember's investment in and
position with the bank preclude you from investing the city's
money in the bank or accepting security from the bank?

2. Would the councilmember's participation in city action
concerning the bank preclude you from taking actions on city
business with the bank?

3. Does the city councilmember's investment in and position
with the bank preclude the city from engaging in other business
relationships with the bank?




1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-91-037
Page 2

4. Do we have any guidelines to avoid even the appearance
of a conflict of interest?

CONCLUSION

1. The councilmember's investment in and position with the
bank does not cause a conflict of interest that precludes you from
doing business with the bank, investing the city's money in the
bank, or accepting security from the bank.

2. The councilmember's participation in city action would
not preclude you from taking actions on city business with the
bank.

3. The city councilmember's investment in and position with
the bank does not preclude the city from engaging in other banking
relationships with the bank.

4. Any additional action to eliminate any further
appearance of a conflict of interest is left to you, the city and
the councilmember.

FACTS

You are the finance director/assistant treasurer for the city
of Healdsburg. In that capacity you are responsible for
investigating and managing the city's idle cash and investment
portfolio. Recently you were approached by the president of
Windsor Oaks National Bank (the "bank") to invest city funds in
bank certificates of deposit. One of your city councilmembers,
Edgar Deas, is a major investor in and director of the bank.

The city's depository resolution requires, dependent on the
particular transaction, two or four signatures, i.e., the city
manager, finance director, mayor or vice mayor. On occasion, but
not typically, the mayor or vice mayor may sign such documents
thus approving such a transaction. Mr. Deas is the vice mayor
this year and would become the mayor next year. According to you,
the signing of such documents is ministerial in nature.

Inasmuch as the bank includes a number of area developers
among its stockholders and on its board of directors, it is likely
that the bank will be the provider of various kinds of security
for city development projects. Normally, the finance director
approves or disapproves such security.

You fear that any direct banking relationship with the bank
creates the appearance of conflict and therefore should be
avoided. On the other hand the city is assertively working with
all banks in the area to focus their efforts under the Community
Reinvestment Act.

Our File No. I-91-037
Page 3

ANALYSIS

1.    Does the councilmember's investment in and position with
the bank preclude you from investing the city's money in the bank
or accepting security from the bank?

     Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.

     The Act provides a four-part test to determine whether a
public official has a conflict of interest in a governmental
decision.  First, is the official considering making, participat-
ing in making, or using his official position to influence a
governmental decision? (Section 87100.)  Second, is it
foreseeable that the decision will affect the official's economic
interests? (Section 87103.)  Third, is the effect of the decision
on the official's economic interests material?  (Id.)  Fourth, is
the effect of the decision on the official's economic interests
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally?  (Id.)
Each of these elements must be met before there is a conflict of
interest.  Here we need to go no further than the second question
to determine that you do not have a conflict of interest.

A.    Making or Participating In A Governmental Decision

     An official makes a governmental decision when he votes, com-
mits his agency to a course of action, enters into a contract, or
appoints someone. (Regulation 18700(b), copy enclosed.)  Deciding
whether to invest city funds with the bank or deciding whether to
accept the bank's security are governmental decisions.

B.    Foreseeable Financial Effect On An Economic Interest

     The second issue is the foreseeability that the decision will
affect your economic interests.  The parameters of a public
official's economic interests are set forth in Section 87103.

     An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, . . . , on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:
     (a)  Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (b)  Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

Our File No. I-91-037
Page 4

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business in
forms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

Section 87103(a)-(e).

In your case, there will be no foreseeable financial effect
on you or your economic interests. From the facts provided, Mr.
Deas may have an economic interest in the decision and may have a
conflict of interest if he participates in the decision to invest
in the bank.3 Mr. Deas's economic interests, however, will not

3 We do not provide advice to third parties about another
person's duties or obligations unless the third party has been
authorized to request advice by the person whose duties or
obligations are in question. (Section 83114, Regulation 18329,
copy enclosed.) Since Mr. Deas has not authorized you to request
advice on his behalf, we cannot provide advice about his
obligations. Please note however that a public official who makes
or participates in a governmental decision or otherwise uses his
official influence where he has a conflict of interest may be
subject to the civil and criminal penalties of the Act, and the
decision may be declared void. (Sections 91000, et seq.) Making
or participating in making a governmental decision is defined in
Regulation 18700 (copy enclosed). Using one's official position
to influence a decision is defined in Regulation 18700.1 (copy
enclosed).

As discussed above, generally speaking, a conflict of
interest may arise if a public official's decision would have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on a business
entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect
investment of $1,000 or more or in which the public official is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management. (Section 87103(a),(d).) For your
information, I have enclosed a copy of Regulation 18702.2 that
addresses when a financial effect upon a business entity is
material and may cause a conflict of interest.

Our File No. I-91-037
Page 5

cause a conflict of interest in your decisions. Thus, Mr. Deas's
economic interests will not preclude you from deciding to invest
funds in the bank or accept the bank's security.

2. Would the councilmember's participation in a city action
preclude you from taking actions on city business with the bank.

Mr. Deas's participation in matters would not preclude
actions on your part.⁴ Again, Mr. Deas's economic interests would
not cause a conflict of interest for you. (Section 87103.)

3. Does the city councilmember's investment in and position
with the bank preclude the city from engaging in other business
relationships with the bank?

As discussed above, the Act prohibits public officials from
taking official actions when they have a conflict of interest.
(See Section 87100.) It does not create an absolute ban on
governmental entities where one of their officials may have a
conflict of interest. Thus, Mr. Deas's economic interest in a
particular governmental decision alone will not preclude others in
the city from making decisions concerning the bank.

4. Do we have any guidelines to avoid even the appearance
of a conflict of interest?

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act are themselves
designed to prevent conflicts of interest and the appearance of
any conflict. Your city may choose on its own to avoid a
relationship with the bank if the prohibitions of the Act do not
eliminate the appearance of a conflict to your satisfaction.
Alternatively, Mr. Deas may choose to relinquish his investment
and position with the bank. Those decisions, however, are left to
you, the city and Mr. Deas.

4 Also note that ministerial actions do not constitute the
making or participating in making of a governmental decision and
thus would not cause a conflict of interest. (Regulation
18700(d), copy enclosed.) For example, as long as the signer of
the check has no discretion, the act is ministerial and would not
trigger a conflict of interest for the signer. (See Klein Advice
Letter, No. A-84-152, (copy enclosed).) We make no determination
here as to whether Mr. Deas's actions are indeed ministerial.

Our File No. I-91-037
Page 6


If you should have any further questions regarding this
matter, please call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: [signature]
Mark T. Morodomi
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:MTM:dg

Enclosures

CONCLUSION: 1. 
The councilmernber' s investment in and position with the
bank does not cause a conflict of interest that precludes you from
doing business lrith the bank, investing the city's rnoney in the
bank, or accepting security fron the bank.
The councilnember's participation in city action would
not precl,ude you from takinq actions on city business with the
bank .
3. 
The city councilmember's investment in and position with
the bank does not preclude the city from engaging in other banking
relationships with the bank.
4. 
Any additional action to elininate any further
appearance oi a conflict of interest is left to you, the city and
the councilrnember.

You are considering investing city funds in a local bank. One of your city councilmembers is a major investor and director of the bank. Does the councilmember's investment in and position with the bank preclude you from investing the city's money in the bank or accepting security from the bank? 2. Would the councilmember's participation in city action concerning the bank preclude you from taking actions on city business with the bank? 3. Does the city councilmember's investment in and position with the bank preclude the city from engaging in other business relationships with the bank? 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 Our File No. I-91-037 Page 2 4. Do we have any guidelines to avoid even the appearance of a conflict of interest? CONCLUSION 1. The councilmember's investment in and position with the bank does not cause a conflict of interest that precludes you from doing business with the bank, investing the city's money in the bank, or accepting security from the bank. 2. The councilmember's participation in city action would not preclude you from taking actions on city business with the bank. 3. The city councilmember's investment in and position with the bank does not preclude the city from engaging in other banking relationships with the bank. 4. Any additional action to eliminate any further appearance of a conflict of interest is left to you, the city and the councilmember.
